# Livepeer developer content: a complete audit across 120+ pages, 30+ repos, and dozens of community resources

**Livepeer's developer documentation ecosystem spans two versioned doc sites (v1 legacy, v2 current), 30+ GitHub repositories, auto-generated SDKs in four languages, 9 batch AI pipelines plus real-time inference, and a growing body of hackathon/community content.** The v2 docs (2025–2026) represent the active, canonical source with ~60 pages covering AI quickstarts, BYOC guides, ComfyStream references, and SDK overviews. The v1 docs (~40+ pages) contain still-valuable pipeline-specific API references and React SDK component documentation that hasn't been fully migrated. Significant content also lives outside the docs site — in GitHub READMEs, Mirror.xyz posts, Encode Club bootcamps, and Livepeer forum governance proposals that contain deep technical detail. Below is the complete audit organized by the eight categories requested.

---

## A. Tutorials that exist today

### Official quickstarts and step-by-step guides

The v2 docs contain four quickstart paths at `v2/developers/get-started/`:

- **AI Jobs Quickstart** (`ai-quickstart`) — sends a first `POST /text-to-image` call via curl to `https://livepeer.studio/api/beta/generate`, covering auth, request/response shape, and error handling. Contains working curl and JSON examples. Noted as still awaiting stakeholder signoff on the default `model_id`.
- **Video Streaming Quickstart** (`video-quickstart`) — creates a first livestream via the Studio API.
- **ComfyStream Quickstart** (`comfystream-quickstart`) — gets ComfyStream running and connected to the Livepeer Network.
- **SDK Gateway Client** (`v2/developers/build/sdk-gateway`) — builds a lightweight gateway client in Python, JavaScript, or mobile using the SDK and a remote signer.

The v2 gateway tutorials section contains two hands-on BYOC tutorials: **"Add AI: BYOC CPU Pipeline"** (`v2/gateways/guides/tutorials/tutorial-2-byoc-cpu-pipeline`) walks through building a custom AI pipeline with PyTrickle and Docker then deploying it, while **"BYOC Smoke-test"** (`v2/gateways/guides/tutorials/byoc-cpu-tutorial`) covers CPU gateway + orchestrator setup from off-chain to on-chain. The orchestrator section has three tutorials: **AI Earning Quickstart**, **Full AI Pipeline Tutorial**, and **Realtime AI Tutorial**.

### External and community tutorials

The most comprehensive hands-on resource is the **Encode Club × Livepeer Real-Time Video AI Bootcamp** (Q1 2025, https://www.encode.club/real-time-video-ai-bootcamp), a multi-week structured curriculum covering ComfyUI environment setup, SD1.5 workflow building, ControlNet for AI-powered video, workflow customization, and deploying as a pipeline. Prizes reached $25k.

The **Eliza AI Agent Tutorial** on Mirror (https://mirror.xyz/agent-spe.eth/oxs8VxEvKN88IeTwD-YQMucoDGqfNRz_mIlbUKkwOTg) provides a complete 10-minute guide to launching an AI agent using Livepeer as the model provider, with full working code including character JSON configuration, `.env` setup, and pnpm commands. SwarmZero's documentation (https://docs.swarmzero.ai/examples/swarms/livepeer-youtube-video-generator-swarm) offers a complete Python tutorial for building an AI agent swarm that generates and uploads YouTube videos using Livepeer's text-to-image and image-to-video APIs. The blog post **"Building Real-Time AI Video Effects with ComfyStream"** (https://blog.livepeer.org/building-real-time-ai-video-effects-with-comfystream/) walks through pipeline architectures like a "Clown Transformation Pipeline" (Input → Depth Map → ControlNet → UNet → VAE → Output).

Older but still referenced tutorials on Mirror.xyz cover Livepeer.js React/Next.js integration, IPFS video export via Postman, and a developer's getting-started guide covering architecture fundamentals. The **awesome-livepeer** list (https://github.com/livepeer/awesome-livepeer) catalogs ~12 demo apps and tutorials including full-stack TikTok/YouTube clones, Arweave/IPFS video upload guides, and token-gated streaming examples.

### Content gap

No standalone, end-to-end "build a production AI app" tutorial exists that takes a developer from zero to deployed application with monitoring. The BYOC and PyTrickle tutorials exist only within the docs — no blog posts or video walkthroughs cover these topics. ComfyStream has a dedicated docs site (https://docs.comfystream.org) but no long-form written tutorial outside the bootcamp.

---

## B. SDK coverage across four languages

### Officially supported SDKs

Livepeer maintains **two parallel SDK families**: Studio SDKs (video streaming + basic AI) and AI-specific SDKs (dedicated AI inference). All are auto-generated by **Speakeasy** from an OpenAPI spec produced by `gen_openapi.py` in the `ai-runner` repo.

| SDK | Package | Version | Registry | Status |
|-----|---------|---------|----------|--------|
| **TypeScript/JS (Studio)** | `livepeer` | 3.5.0 | npm | Active |
| **React UI Kit** | `@livepeer/react` | 4.3.2 | npm | Active |
| **Python (Studio)** | `livepeer` | 0.3.0 | PyPI | Active |
| **Go (Studio)** | `github.com/livepeer/livepeer-go` | — | Go modules | Active |
| **TypeScript/JS (AI)** | `@livepeer/ai` | Alpha | npm | Active |
| **Python (AI)** | `livepeer-ai` | Alpha | PyPI | Active |
| **Go (AI)** | `github.com/livepeer/livepeer-ai-go` | Alpha | Go modules | Active |

The three AI SDKs expose an identical **10-method `generate` namespace**: `textToImage`, `imageToImage`, `imageToVideo`, `upscale`, `audioToText`, `segmentAnything2`, `llm`, `imageToText`, `liveVideoToVideo`, and `textToSpeech`. Default gateway endpoint is `https://dream-gateway.livepeer.cloud`, with `https://livepeer.studio/api/beta/generate` as the Studio-hosted alternative.

All SDKs authenticate via **HTTP Bearer token** (API key from Livepeer Studio dashboard). The AI SDKs support configurable retries with exponential backoff, streaming file uploads, custom HTTP clients (httpx for Python, native Fetch for JS), and debug logging. Error types include `HTTPError` (400/401/500), `HTTPValidationError` (422), and `SDKError` (4XX/5XX).

### React component library

The `@livepeer/react` package (from `livepeer/ui-kit` monorepo, **71 stars**) provides composable video primitives: `Player` component with HLS/WebRTC support, `Broadcast` component for in-browser streaming, WAI-ARIA accessibility, keyboard shortcuts, and PictureInPicture. The monorepo includes Next.js App Router and Pages Router examples plus a PubNub integration example.

### Deprecated and archived SDKs

`@livepeer/webrtmp-sdk` (0.2.5) is deprecated in favor of the Broadcast SDK. `@livepeer/react-native` (0.0.1) is deprecated — React Native developers are directed to `react-native-video` for HLS and `react-native-webrtc` for WebRTC. `@livepeer/sdk` (1.0.1-alpha.5, last published 5 years ago) for smart contract interactions is archived. The `livepeer/livepeer-monorepo` containing the old livepeer.js is also archived.

---

## C. The AI inference API surface

### Batch AI endpoints

All batch AI pipelines are accessible via **POST** requests to the gateway. The canonical base URL for Studio-hosted access is `https://livepeer.studio/api/beta/generate`. Nine pipelines are available:

| Pipeline | Endpoint | Warm Model | Min VRAM |
|----------|----------|-----------|----------|
| Text to Image | `/text-to-image` | SG161222/RealVisXL_V4.0_Lightning | 24 GB |
| Image to Image | `/image-to-image` | timbrooks/instruct-pix2pix | 20 GB |
| Image to Video | `/image-to-video` | stabilityai/stable-video-diffusion-img2vid-xt | — |
| Image to Text | `/image-to-text` | Salesforce/blip-image-captioning-large | 4 GB |
| Audio to Text | `/audio-to-text` | openai/whisper-large-v3 | 12 GB |
| Text to Speech | `/text-to-speech` | Parler-TTS based | 12 GB |
| Upscale | `/upscale` | stabilityai/stable-diffusion-x4-upscaler | 24 GB |
| Segment Anything 2 | `/segment-anything-2` | facebook/sam2-hiera-large | 6 GB |
| LLM | `/llm` | meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | 8 GB |

**Warm models** process immediately; cold models incur **30 seconds to several minutes** load time. Custom Hugging Face model IDs are accepted via the `model_id` parameter. LoRA models are supported. The LLM pipeline uses the **OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` format** via an Ollama-based runner, supporting Llama, Mistral, Gemma, Qwen, and other Ollama-compatible models.

### Real-time vs batch

**Batch pipelines** use standard REST request-response: send an image/text/audio, get back a result. **Real-time AI** (`live-video-to-video`) is fundamentally different — it uses the **trickle streaming protocol** over WebRTC, not REST. Real-time inference is served by ComfyStream and processes live webcam/video feeds frame-by-frame at up to **25 FPS** on an RTX 4090. This pipeline is not callable via the standard REST API; it requires a trickle streaming connection either through `@muxionlabs/byoc-sdk` (browser WebRTC) or direct trickle client integration.

### Authentication and pricing

Authentication uses Bearer tokens from the Livepeer Studio dashboard. Two key types exist: **backend API keys** (server-side, maximum security) and **CORS-enabled keys** (allow direct browser requests; asset/stream IDs become the security boundary). A public gateway at `livepeer.cloud` is available for free testing but not production use.

Pricing follows a tiered model for video: **Free** (1,000 transcoding min/month, 30 concurrent viewers), **Growth** ($100/month minimum, $0.33/60 min transcoding, 50K concurrent viewers), and **Enterprise** ($2,500/month annual commit, custom pricing). AI inference pricing is **orchestrator-set** at approximately **1.9×10⁻⁸ USD per output pixel** for text-to-image. Rate limits are configurable and visible in the Studio dashboard.

### OpenAPI spec

The OpenAPI specification is generated from FastAPI routes in the `ai-runner` repository via `gen_openapi.py`, producing `openapi_gateway.json`. Go client bindings for go-livepeer are generated from this spec via `make ai_worker_codegen`. The v2 docs reference this spec at `v2/gateways/resources/technical/api-reference/AI-API/ai`. The v1 docs contain per-pipeline API reference pages under `/ai/api-reference/`.

---

## D. BYOC: how it works technically

### Architecture

BYOC (Bring Your Own Container) lets developers deploy **any custom AI model** on the Livepeer network inside a Docker container. There is no standalone `livepeer/BYOC` repository — BYOC is implemented as a `byoc/` directory within `go-livepeer` (containing `byoc.go`, `job_auth.go`, `job_gateway.go`, `trickle.go`, `payment.go`, `stream_orchestrator.go`, `stream_gateway.go`) and documented at `v2/developers/build/byoc`.

### Container interface contract

Every BYOC container must use **PyTrickle's `StreamServer`**, which automatically exposes four REST endpoints:

| Endpoint | Method | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `/api/stream/start` | POST | Start stream session (receives `subscribe_url`, `publish_url`, `params`) |
| `/api/stream/params` | POST | Update parameters mid-stream |
| `/api/stream/status` | GET | Check session status |
| `/api/stream/stop` | POST | Stop current session |

### Building a BYOC container

The documented workflow has four steps: **(1)** Implement a `FrameProcessor` subclass in Python via PyTrickle — this is the core class where developers write their AI inference logic. **(2)** Build a Docker container (base image `nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-cudnn8-runtime-ubuntu22.04` for GPU workloads or `python:3.11-slim` for CPU). **(3)** Test locally with the `http-trickle` server. **(4)** Deploy to the Livepeer network via go-livepeer orchestrator with `-byoc -byocContainerURL -byocModelID` flags.

### Client-side connection

On the consumer side, `@muxionlabs/byoc-sdk` provides a browser WebRTC client:

```javascript
import { BYOCClient } from '@muxionlabs/byoc-sdk';
const client = new BYOCClient({
  gatewayUrl: 'https://<livepeer-gateway-url>',
  capability: 'live-video-to-video',
});
await client.startStream({ videoElement, params: { /* your params */ } });
```

**MuxionLabs** is the restructured name of the AI SPE (Special Purpose Entity) that develops BYOC tooling. The `@muxionlabs/byoc-sdk` package was not found in public npm search results — it may be distributed privately or under a different published name. BYOC was **hardened to production-grade in Phase 4 (January 2026)**, with production workloads running via Embody SPE and Streamplace.

### Batch vs streaming BYOC

The current BYOC implementation is **streaming-first** — containers process frames in real-time via the trickle protocol. Batch BYOC (send a single request, get a single response) is not explicitly documented as a separate mode; the primary use case is continuous frame processing for live-video-to-video workflows.

---

## E. ComfyStream's current feature set

### Core capabilities

ComfyStream (https://github.com/livepeer/ComfyStream, dedicated docs at https://docs.comfystream.org) is the **primary real-time AI video processing engine** for the Livepeer network. It takes ComfyUI workflow JSON files (API format) and applies them to live webcam/video streams via WebRTC. Key features as of Phase 4 (January 2026):

- **Pipeline modes**: image-to-image, video-to-video, audio processing, data-channel output
- **Node ecosystem**: StreamDiffusion nodes, AudioTranscription nodes, LoadTensor/SaveTensor I/O
- **Workflow format**: Standard ComfyUI API format JSON — single primary input node (PrimaryInputLoadImage or LoadImage), single output via PreviewImage/SaveImage, automatically converted to LoadTensor/SaveTensor at runtime
- **Frame rate benchmarks**: Up to **25 FPS** on NVIDIA RTX 4090 (CUDA 12.5, PyTorch 2.5.1+cu121)
- **Configuration params**: Configurable frame rates (1-60 FPS, default 24), media ports, TURN server support

### Supported models and workflows

Any ComfyUI-compatible model chain works, including **StreamDiffusion** (core real-time diffusion inference), **ControlNet** (depth-guided generation), **IPAdapter**, **FaceID**, **LoRAs**, **SuperResolution**, **Whisper** (audio transcription), and **Gemma** (video understanding). The `livepeer/ComfyUI-Stream-Pack` repository (20 stars) extends ComfyUI with streaming-specific custom nodes organized into Foundation, Light, and Input/Output categories.

### Deployment options

ComfyStream offers five deployment paths: **Docker DevContainer** (pre-configured), **Docker image** (`livepeer/comfystream`), **RunPod template** (one-click deploy via `livepeer-comfystream`), **Tensordock** (automated Python deployment script), and **Ansible Playbook** (cloud-agnostic). It's also pip-installable: `pip install git+https://github.com/livepeer/comfystream.git` (requires Miniconda, Python 3.12, PyTorch). A Next.js UI ships in the `ui/` directory.

### Tutorials and workshops

The **ComfyUI Live Video Hacker Program** (https://www.livepeer.org/comfyui-live-video-hacker-program) ran two cohorts (Demo Day January 31, 2025; second cohort late February 2025) as an exclusive developer program with grants for open-sourcing workflows. The Encode Club bootcamp (Q1 2025) covered ComfyStream workflow building across four structured lessons. The blog post on "Building Real-Time AI Video Effects with ComfyStream" provides architectural walkthroughs with pipeline diagrams.

---

## F. PyTrickle and the newer developer SDKs

### PyTrickle

PyTrickle (https://github.com/livepeer/pytrickle, created July 2025, **3 stars, 20 open issues**) is a high-performance Python framework for real-time video/audio streaming over the **trickle protocol** (`j0sh/http-trickle`). It is the **critical bridge** between custom AI models and the Livepeer network. Architecture components include `StreamServer` (HTTP server with REST API), `FrameProcessor` (base class for AI processors), `TrickleClient` (direct client), and `TrickleProtocol` (high-level protocol).

Key features: async/await processing with asyncio, **PyTorch tensor-based video frames** (`VideoFrame`, `AudioFrame`), automatic CUDA GPU memory management, real-time parameter updates mid-stream, configurable frame rates (1-60 FPS), audio support (mono/stereo/multi-channel), built-in monitoring (FPS, latency, memory, errors), and auto-reconnection. Data flow: `Input Stream → Decoder → FrameProcessor → Encoder → Output Stream`. Requires Python 3.8+, PyTorch, FFmpeg. Installation via `pip install -e .`.

### @muxionlabs/byoc-sdk

Referenced in official Livepeer docs as the client-side SDK for connecting browsers to BYOC containers via WebRTC. Handles data-channel support and includes React hooks. Part of Phase 4 deliverables confirmed in the AI SPE Phase 4 Retrospective (https://forum.livepeer.org/t/ai-spe-phase-4-retrospective/3208). Not found in public npm — likely distributed via MuxionLabs channels or not yet publicly published.

### NaaP (Node as a Platform)

A newly emerged project (https://github.com/livepeer/naap, created February 2026, **4 stars**) — an open-source plugin-based platform built on Next.js 15 that hosts independent plugins as UMD bundles. Contains 12 plugins, a `BYOCAdapter`, and uses React 19, Express backends, and Prisma ORM with PostgreSQL. Developers scaffold plugins with `npx naap-plugin create my-plugin`. This is the **emerging management/operations platform** for the Livepeer AI network.

---

## G. Production and deployment guidance

### Going to production

The v2 docs provide a clear production path through the **Developer Journey** page (`v2/developers/developer-journey`), which maps five builder profiles (App Dev, AI Dev, Gateway Operator, GPU Operator, Protocol Dev) to a zero-to-hero progression matrix. The **Developer Stack** concept page (`v2/developers/concepts/developer-stack`) provides a decision table for choosing between Studio, Daydream, AI Gateway API, ComfyStream, and Protocol layer based on use case.

For video streaming, production involves: creating streams via Studio API, configuring transcoding profiles, setting up webhooks for event monitoring, implementing JWT or webhook-based access control, and optionally configuring multistream targets. For AI inference, production means: using the Studio-hosted gateway (not the free public gateway), implementing proper Bearer token authentication with backend API keys, and handling cold model load times by preferring warm models.

### Monitoring and debugging

The v2 gateway docs include a dedicated **Monitoring Setup** page (`v2/gateways/guides/monitoring-and-tooling/monitoring-setup`) covering Prometheus and Grafana configuration. The orchestrator section has a parallel monitoring page (`v2/orchestrators/setup/r-monitor`). PyTrickle includes built-in monitoring for FPS, latency, memory usage, and error rates. The **Orchestrator Selection & Tiering** page covers performance scoring, blocklists, and failover strategies. Livepeer Studio provides a status page at https://status.livepeer.studio/.

### Error patterns and troubleshooting

SDK error types are well-documented: `HTTPError` (400 bad request, 401 unauthorized, 500 server error), `HTTPValidationError` (422 validation failure), and `SDKError` (catch-all for 4XX/5XX). Common issues include cold model timeouts (30s–minutes), CORS configuration for browser-side API keys, and trickle protocol connection issues for real-time AI. The AI Quickstart page notes error handling patterns explicitly. The forum contains performance tuning threads (e.g., "ComfyStream SD1.5 Workflow Performance Tuning" identifies CPU bottlenecks on RunPod).

### Cost guidance

The three-tier pricing model (Free sandbox at 1,000 min/month, Growth at $100/month minimum, Enterprise at $2,500/month) is documented at https://livepeer.studio/pricing. AI inference costs are orchestrator-determined, approximately **$0.019 per megapixel** for text-to-image. The gateway business case page (`v2/gateways/guides/operator-considerations/business-case`) covers revenue economics and self-hosted vs hosted tradeoffs. Streaming credits are available via application.

---

## H. V1 content worth migrating to v2

### High-priority migration candidates

The v1 AI pipeline documentation (`v1/ai/pipelines/`) contains **per-pipeline technical pages** for all 9 pipelines with supported models, warm model lists, usage examples, and code samples. These are the most content-rich pages in v1 and have no exact v2 equivalent — the v2 AI API reference pages exist under the gateway section but are structured differently. The v1 AI API reference pages (`v1/ai/api-reference/`) provide endpoint-specific documentation with request/response examples.

The v1 AI SDK docs (`v1/ai/sdks/javascript`, `v1/ai/sdks/python`) contain code examples for each pipeline that could be updated and migrated. The v1 developer guides under `/v1/developers/guides/` cover practical recipes: **in-browser broadcasting** (WebRTC), **stream event webhooks**, **asset upload**, **encrypted assets**, **multistream setup**, **JWT access control**, **webhook access control**, **asset playback**, and **engagement analytics** — many with working code.

### React SDK documentation

The v1 React SDK section (`v1/sdks/react/`) is extensive with per-component documentation: Player (with React Native support), Broadcast, Controls, PictureInPicture, Audio, and hooks like `useCreateStream` and `useUpdateStream`. This component-level documentation doesn't exist in v2 and represents significant content worth preserving, though it references the older API (`createReactClient`, `studioProvider`, `LivepeerConfig`).

### Tutorials worth updating

Two v1 tutorials warrant migration with updates: **"Upload & Playback Videos on IPFS"** (`v1/developers/tutorials/upload-playback-videos-on-ipfs`) and **"Upload & Playback via 4everland"** — both demonstrate decentralized storage integration patterns still relevant to the Web3 developer audience. The v1 orchestrator AI docs (`v1/ai/orchestrators/`) cover model configuration, orchestrator startup, and remote AI worker attachment — content that has been substantially expanded in v2 but may contain details not yet migrated.

### What's already well-covered in v2

The v2 docs already have strong coverage of: BYOC development (`v2/developers/build/byoc`), ComfyStream reference (`v2/developers/build/comfystream`), the developer journey and stack concepts, gateway operations, orchestrator setup and AI pipeline configuration, and Livepeer Studio API reference with full endpoint documentation. The v2 developer guides page (`v2/developers/guides/developer-guides`) curates links to both official and community content, functioning as a discovery hub.

---

## Ecosystem map and confidence assessment

### Repository landscape (30+ repos, key ones)

| Repository | Stars | Language | Role | Activity |
|------------|-------|----------|------|----------|
| `go-livepeer` | 582 | Go | Protocol node (v0.8.10) | Very active (April 2026) |
| `lpms` | 283 | Go | Media server library | Active |
| `protocol` | 153 | Solidity | Smart contracts | Active |
| `studio` | 85 | TypeScript | Platform/dashboard | Active |
| `ui-kit` | 71 | TypeScript | React video components | Active (Sept 2025) |
| `awesome-livepeer` | 52 | — | Community resource list | Active (Oct 2025) |
| `docs` | 43 | MDX | Documentation source | Active |
| `ai-runner` | 24 | Python | AI inference runtime (v0.14.1) | Active (Dec 2025) |
| `ComfyStream` | — | Python | Real-time AI video | Active |
| `ComfyUI-Stream-Pack` | 20 | Python | Custom ComfyUI nodes | Active |
| `pytrickle` | 3 | Python | Trickle protocol bridge | Active (20 open issues) |
| `naap` | 4 | TypeScript | Plugin platform | Very new (Feb 2026) |

### Confidence levels

**High confidence**: SDK versions, API endpoints, pipeline types, BYOC architecture, pricing tiers, authentication methods, repository structures — all verified from multiple sources (docs, GitHub, npm/PyPI).

**Medium confidence**: `@muxionlabs/byoc-sdk` availability (referenced in docs but not found in public npm), exact current warm model lists (may change frequently), real-time frame rate benchmarks (hardware-dependent), NaaP maturity level (very new project).

**Potentially outdated content**: All v1 docs pages (legacy but still accessible), React SDK component docs (reference older API patterns), Mirror.xyz tutorials from 2022-2023 (pre-AI pivot, use deprecated livepeer.js patterns), Encode Club 2022 hackathon materials (pre-AI, focused on video streaming only).

**Notable gaps**: No standalone PyTrickle tutorial or blog post exists. No BYOC blog post or video walkthrough. No end-to-end "build a production AI app" tutorial. No pricing calculator or cost estimation guide. No explicit rate limit documentation beyond "check dashboard." The `@muxionlabs/byoc-sdk` needs public documentation. ComfyStream's dedicated docs site (docs.comfystream.org) exists but is maintained separately from the main docs.
